title: Coil-Built Mushroom Sculpture in Air-Dry Clay
subtitle: A freestanding mushroom with a coil stem and draped cap
excerpt: Build a freestanding mushroom from air-dry clay using coil-building for the stem and a draped slab for the cap. No kiln required. Paint with acrylics after PVA sealing for a finished garden or shelf decoration.
sourceNotes: Technique derived from standard air-dry coil-building and draping methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: A long roll of clay, similar to a thick sausage shape, used to build up the walls of hand-built pieces layer by layer
The method of joining two pieces of clay by scratching the surfaces to be joined (scoring) and wetting them with liquid clay (slip) before pressing together
The stage at which clay holds its shape and no longer sticks to your hands, but still contains moisture and can be carved or smoothed
A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness, used here to form the mushroom cap

content.text: This mushroom sculpture combines two fundamental hand-building techniques:
coil
building for the hollow stem and a draped
slab
for the cap. The two parts are joined using the
score and slip
method, then left to dry before painting with acrylics. No kiln needed. The finished sculpture stands freestanding on a shelf or windowsill.

materials.name: Acrylic paint (white, red, brown, as preferred)
Small bowl or ball (approx 10 cm) for cap former
Water in a small dish (for slip and smoothing)

content.text: Preparation
Cover your rounded bowl or ball former with cling film and set it aside.
Mix a small amount of slip by dissolving a pea-sized piece of clay in water until you have a smooth, paste-like consistency. You will use this to join pieces.
Knead the clay for 2 minutes until smooth.
Building the stem
Roll a
coil
about 25 cm long and 12 mm in diameter. Keep the diameter even by rolling from the centre outward with both palms flat on the work surface.
Coil the roll into a tight cylinder about 6 cm tall and 4 cm in diameter. Each layer should sit directly on top of the last.

Use the scoring tool to scratch the inner joins between coil layers, then press and smooth each join on the inside and outside using a damp fingertip. This blends the coils into a solid wall. The finished stem walls should be around 8 mm thick.
Close the base by rolling a small disc of clay 4 mm thick and attaching it to the bottom of the stem using score and slip. Smooth the join inside and out.
Set the stem aside on baking paper and allow it to reach
leather-hard
stage before attaching the cap. This takes roughly 3-4 hours.
Making and attaching the cap
Roll a
slab
5 mm thick, large enough to drape over your rounded former. A circle of about 18 cm covers a 10 cm former with an overhang for the cap brim.
Lay the slab gently over the cling-filmed former and ease it into a smooth dome. The brim will flare or droop naturally at the edges; leave it as it falls for a natural mushroom look.
Allow the cap to reach leather-hard stage on the former (3-4 hours). Then lift it off carefully and set it flat-side up on the work surface.
Use the
score and slip
method to attach the top of the stem to the centre of the cap underside. Score both surfaces, apply slip to each, press firmly together, and smooth the join with a damp fingertip.
Drying, sealing, and painting

Once assembled, place the mushroom upright (cap on top) on baking paper. Dry slowly at room temperature. Cover loosely with a plastic bag for the first 12 hours to prevent cracking at the stem-cap join. Full drying takes 3 days.
When fully dry and pale, apply two coats of diluted PVA (1:1 with water), allowing 1 hour between coats. This seals the surface and provides a good base for acrylic paint.
Paint with acrylics. A classic red cap with white spots takes two coats of red, then dots of white painted on with the end of a pencil or a liner brush. Paint the stem off-white or pale cream.
Apply one further coat of diluted PVA over the dried paint to protect the colour.
Variations
Make a family of three mushrooms at different heights for a grouped shelf display.
Texture the cap with a stamp or lace before draping for added surface detail.
Build the stem as a solid roll rather than hollow coils for a smaller, more compact mushroom.
Leave unpointed for a natural clay look sealed with matte PVA rather than painted.

items.problem: The cap cracked where it meets the stem
items.cause: Cap and stem dried at different rates before assembly
items.fix: Make sure both pieces are at the same leather-hard stage before joining. Slow drying with a plastic bag after assembly.

items.problem: The stem is wobbly or has gaps between coils
items.cause: Coil joins were not smoothed thoroughly
items.fix: Blend every coil join on both the inside and outside of the cylinder. A damp finger running along the join in one smooth stroke works well.

items.problem: The cap collapsed or lost its dome shape
items.cause: Removed from the former before leather-hard
items.fix: Leave on the former until the clay is firm and no longer cool to the touch. Return to the former and wait another 2 hours.

items.problem: Paint peeled off the dried clay
items.cause: Acrylic was applied directly to unsealed clay
items.fix: Always apply the PVA sealing coats before painting. The seal gives the paint a stable, smooth surface to grip.
